dialogue.utterance: Caller 32 Male person down reported.
Hi, I'm Sam, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Uh, he fell and hit his head, then he started seizing.
Sir, can you open your eyes for me?
He opened his eyes but didn't look at me when I touched his shoulder.
Can you tell me your name?
His arms moved into a decorticate position when we tried to move him.
Airway is patent and self-maintained, no blood in the mouth.
Breathing is normal in rate, adequate depth, and no signs of distress noted. You're not using accessory muscles, correct?
I've got a strong and regular radial pulse. Skin is warm, dry, and normal in color.
Sir, can you tell me about the pain you're feeling?
It's really bad, right here at the back of my head.
Are you taking any medications?
Do you have any past medical history I should know about?
I have seizures from alcohol withdrawal. I have Hepatitis C and liver disease.
What did you last eat or drink?
He said he had three 16oz beers and he does this every day.
What happened just before you fell?
He was walking around for about an hour, drinking alcohol.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how bad is your pain?
10, and it's in the back of my head and now my neck.
Your skin is dry and warm. Your head is soaked, possibly from rain, but not diaphoretic.
There is blood and a laceration at the back of your head. It seems swollen.
Your airway appears clear and self-maintained, no obstructions noted in your mouth.
Your breathing is regular, about 14 breaths a minute, with no signs of distress. No pain noted with inspiration or expiration.
Let me check your arms and legs. Can you move them all?
We're going to check your oxygen level now.
Your oxygen is at 100%.
We're going to check your blood sugar now.
Blood sugar is 114 mg/dL.
Pulse is at 99 beats per minute.
Your breathing is at 14 breaths per minute.
Blood pressure is 115/79.
EKG shows a sinus rhythm.
We're going to attach a heart monitor for cardiac rhythm checks and initiate 4 lead ECG monitoring.
We're going to give a medication to help control the seizure. It's midazolam 5mg intranasal.
We've just administered midazolam IN now, as part of the medical seizure protocol.
We're going to cover the laceration with gauze and apply cling.
We found it difficult to apply the cervical collar initially. We'll try again once the medication begins to wear off.
Suspected alcohol withdrawal seizure. Protocol 3-12 activated.
Let's take your oxygen and glucose again.
Your oxygen is at 99% now.
Blood sugar is reading at 0, but the value may be inaccurate.
Pulse is at 87, and your breathing appears to be 18 per minute now.
Blood pressure is 95 over 66.
Your cardiac monitor is stable, and you're awake and alert (AOx4) with no arrhythmias noted.
Your GCS is now 15 with an eye opening of 4, verbal of 5, and a motor of 6.
We've also applied an end-tidal CO2 monitor to monitor for possible issues.
We are going to VCU Health.
You're secured on the stretcher. Are you okay with us moving?
You're warmed and placed on a stretcher. Let's get you inside the ambulance.
The medication might take some effect. How is the pain now?
It's still a 10, but my neck started hurting too.
Okay, let's apply the cervical collar now. Can you hold still?
Collar applied. You remain alert and oriented as we transport.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive. Eyes appear to be PERRL.
Things going okay? Let me take a quick look at your vitals again.
Blood sugar still reads at 0, and oxygen is 99.
We're still not seeing signs of distress even with the new neck complaint. Breath sounds remain clear bilaterally.
After the medications wore slightly off, we applied the cervical collar. It was a smooth process and you've not had another episode since.
Things going okay? Let me take a quick look at your vitals again.
Blood sugar still reads at 0, and oxygen is 99.
You're stable and we're about to wrap up with this unit.
I owe everything to you guys. I just wasn't thinking when those bozos jumped the f out of no where. You got it from here? I'm exhausted.
We've got it. VCU will be your next stop and they'll help you stabilize your alcohol withdrawal. You're welcome.
Any new symptoms while we're on the way? Neck pain persisting?
Yeah. Pain is still a 10, hasn't gone down yet. But I haven't relapsed or anything.
No seizure signs detected on the monitor. That's our goal-prevent anything from flaring back up.
Any changes in your dizziness or vision?
Nope. Eyes just a little tired, but good.
Pupils still PERRL, no signs of falling back into an altered level.
Are you still experiencing pain in the same spot on the back of your head?
Yep, and now the lower back from the stretcher.
We recorded the head laceration and other findings in case the doctor needs more details.
Let's double-check that everything stays in place during the ride. No movement complaints from others?
No, he just collapsed without warning. It looked like a seizure.
Thank you for confirming. We'll make sure to inform the hospital about that.
Any prior injuries you remember being associated with the fall?
No it wasn't an injury. Definitely a seizure this time.
What caused the fall then?
He was walking the sidewalk, had a little wobble, and next thing we saw was his head on the ground.
Then we're lucky he didn't take another hit. He's definitely lucky you called 911.
Head wound is stable and covered. Cervical collar secured correctly. Vital signs have not shown progressive concern. We'll keep the monitor and you on board.
Vital signs and level of alertness have not declined. You remain a stable transport candidate with no acute findings.
You're set to go and should expect further support in the emergency department.
Thanks for giving us a clear picture of your condition. We'll get you to care quickly.
